item.title: What's new in v0.5.0
item.description: Gradgen v0.5.0 adds embeddable Rust code generation improvements, shorter OCP-generated code, and better support for OpEn integration.
item.content:encoded:
Gradgen v0.5.0 brings significant changes and new functionality. Most importantly, it brings us very close to a full integration with OpEn, this means that:

  • We'll be generating pure Rust code, fully embeddable (#[no-std]), safe, with support for both single and double precision arithmetic
  • The generated code for optimal control problems will be significantly shorter. This will decrease significantly the compilation times (from minutes to seconds)

Gradgen v0.5.0 can be installed using pip:

pip install gradgen==0.5.0

Upcoming developments:

  • Improved documentation and examples, including Python notebooks on Google Colab and demos
  • Benchmarks
  • Support for the forward-backward envelope in optimal control problems

item.title: API docs
item.description: The API docs of gradgen are now available here. The API docs have been generated automatically from the docstrings in the code using Sphinx.
item.content:encoded: The API docs of gradgen are now available here. The API docs have been generated automatically from the docstrings in the code using Sphinx.

item.title: Gradgen revival
item.description: Welcome to this brand new website of gradgen: your Python module for automatic differentiation and truly embedded Rust code generation.
item.content:encoded:
Welcome to this brand new website of gradgen: your Python module for automatic differentiation and truly embedded Rust code generation.

Gradgen v0.3 brings two significant changes:

  1. It has been redesigned to generate Rust crates, optionally marked with #[no_std] so you can use them directly in your embedded applications
  2. It comes with specialised code generation mechanisms for optimal control leading to Rust code that does not increase in size with the prediction horizon

We are already working on gradgen v0.3 which will bring one more significant change: support for high-order functions like map, zip, and reduce, which will improve code generation even more.

Our goal is to exploit the structure of a function, its gradient, and Hessian to generate more efficient, elegant, and fast Rust code.
